content.problemStatement:
Chef has a sequence **A** containing **N** integers **A1, A2, ..., AN**.

Chef is playing a game with this sequence. In this game, he may perform the following operation any number of times (including zero): Choose an arbitrary pair of elements from the sequence such that their sum is even, delete these two elements from the sequence and insert their sum into the sequence instead.

The goal of the game is to minimise the number of elements in the sequence.

Help Chef to play the game and find the minimum possible size of the sequence at the end of the game!
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains a single integer N. The second line contains N space-separated integers A1, A2, ..., AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the minimum possible final size of the sequence.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, we have two elements: 1 and 2. Since their sum is 3 (odd), we cannot merge them. So, the minimum possible final size of the sequence is 2. For the second test case, we have five elements: 7, 4, 3, 2, and 6. We can merge the even elements (4, 2, and 6) into one element, and the odd elements (7 and 3) into one element. So, the minimum possible final size of the sequence is 1.
We can merge the even elements (2 and 4) into one element, and the odd elements (1 and 3) into one element. So, the minimum possible final size of the sequence is 1.
We can merge the even element (2) with one of the odd elements (1 or 3), but we cannot merge the remaining two elements. So, the minimum possible final size of the sequence is 2.

solution.approach: To minimize the number of elements in the sequence, we need to merge as many pairs of elements as possible. We can do this by counting the number of even and odd elements in the sequence. If the number of odd elements is even, we can merge all of them into one element. If the number of odd elements is odd, we can merge all but one of them into one element. The number of even elements can be merged in pairs, leaving at most one even element if the number of even elements is odd.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T*N) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the maximum number of elements in a sequence
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — for storing the sequence of elements
